one_line_takeaway: Safety-alignment fine-tuning's utility cost is concentrated in a small, identifiable subset of attention heads (mostly in middle-to-deep layers); a pre-alignment 'Conflict Score' combining gradient opposition and functional-ablation sensitivity reliably (Pearson r up to 1.00) predicts this cost and lets sparse fine-tuning of only the low-conflict heads recover most of the lost general capability (e.g. +9.45 MMLU points on Llama) while matching full-model safety-alignment gains.
key_finding: On Llama, CAST-SFT (Safe Zone) raised MMLU accuracy by +9.45 points over Full-SFT (55.73 vs 46.28) while achieving comparable or better safety (Avg Safe 92.62 vs 90.61) -- Table 1. Across all three models, CAST-SFT (Safe Zone) dominated the safety-utility Pareto frontier versus Full-SFT, Random-SFT(25%), and CAST-SFT (Risky Zone) (Figure 3); e.g. on Mistral, updating the Risky Zone caused a catastrophic utility collapse (MMLU 27.78, CSQA 25.72) versus Safe Zone (MMLU 47.62, CSQA 56.35) -- Table 1. The Conflict Score C(h) is strongly predictive of realized alignment cost: aggregated over 4 conflict buckets, Pearson r for MMLU-CR was 0.99 (Llama), 1.00 (Qwen), 0.94 (Mistral), and Pearson r for UCR was 0.95 (Llama), 0.73 (Qwen), 0.95 (Mistral), with Spearman rho >= 0.80 in all cases (Figures 5-6). CAST also synergizes with the geometric baseline PCGrad: CAST+PCG (Safe Zone) dominated the Pareto frontier over Full-SFT and Full-PCGrad (Table 2, Figure 4), and PCGrad rescued some Risky-Zone degradation on Mistral MMLU (27.78% -> 51.43%) but Safe Zone still reached the Pareto peak (53.46%). An ablation (Table 8, appendix) showed the unified metric C(h)=O(h)*S(h) is necessary: decoupled O-only or S-only metrics were unstable predictors (e.g., S failed to predict MMLU degradation in Qwen, r=-0.10; O negatively correlated with MMLU-CR in Llama, r=-0.13), whereas the unified score kept r in [0.73, 1.00] across all models. Qualitative appendix examples on GSM8K show Full-SFT models exhibiting Reasoning Collapse (skipping CoT, hallucinating an answer of 66 instead of the correct 81 for a frog-counting problem) and Over-Refusal (refusing a benign babysitting-earnings word problem), both of which CAST (Safe Zone) avoided while producing correct step-by-step answers.
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution is Conflict-Aware Sparse Tuning (CAST), a two-stage framework for safety alignment fine-tuning that (1) computes a pre-alignment, head-level 'Conflict Score' C(h) = O(h)*S(h) for each attention head in the Query-projection (Wq) matrices, combining Optimization Conflict O(h) (cosine distance between gradients from a harmful-prompt loss and a utility loss) and Functional Sensitivity S(h) (an exponential function of the percentile-rank gap between zero-shot-ablation effects on utility accuracy and on safety refusal), and (2) uses this Conflict Map to decide which heads to update during LoRA-based safety fine-tuning, e.g. sparsely updating only the bottom-25% 'Safe Zone' heads (lowest conflict) while freezing the rest, versus updating the top-25% 'Risky Zone' heads. The paper argues (and empirically shows via a 'Budget-Matched' controlled protocol with equal parameter counts across bucket choices) that safety-utility conflict during alignment fine-tuning is concentrated in a small, structurally localized subset of attention heads rather than diffused globally across the Transformer, and that this diagnostic score is predictive of realized alignment-induced utility loss.
threat_model: This is not an attacker/defender security paper in the adversarial-attack sense; there is no threat model involving an adversary crafting inputs to fool a defense. The 'safety' axis evaluated is standard LLM safety alignment: refusal of harmful requests measured via Defense Success Rate on WildJailbreak (adversarial and vanilla harmful prompts), generalization to WildGuard and DAN jailbreak prompt sets. The paper treats jailbreak-style prompt sets purely as an evaluation benchmark for whether the fine-tuned model refuses harmful content, not as a novel attack technique being proposed or defended against. The practical concern addressed is the 'alignment tax': safety fine-tuning that degrades general (reasoning/knowledge) capability, including qualitatively documented failure modes of naive/global safety fine-tuning: Reasoning Collapse (loss of chain-of-thought structure) and Safety Over-refusal/misgeneralization (refusing benign prompts, e.g., a benign GSM8K-style babysitting-earnings word problem).
limitations: The paper explicitly states three limitations in its own Limitations section: (1) Structural Scope -- the method targets only Query Projection (Wq) matrices, not MLPs, which hold the majority of parameters and are key for factual knowledge storage; (2) Static Diagnosis -- CAST uses a fixed, pre-alignment conflict map and assumes conflict regions remain stable, potentially missing 'conflict drift' where high-conflict zones shift during extensive fine-tuning; (3) Task Dependence -- the diagnosis relies on specific calibration sets (MMLU/WildJailbreak) that may not capture conflict patterns in specialized domains like coding or creative writing. The paper also notes (in the ablation discussion) that data-sensitivity experiments found the diagnosis robust with as few as 100 calibration samples, but that domain transfer reveals task-specific conflict topologies, necessitating domain-aware diagnosis.

datasets_used: MMLU (Hendrycks et al. 2021) - 500-sample calibration set Dutil, and full 14,042-example test set for utility eval

WildJailbreak (Jiang et al. 2024) - 500 vanilla harmful prompts for Dsafe calibration, and 10,000-sample Dalign for safety-alignment training (2,500 each: vanilla harmful, adversarial harmful, vanilla benign, adversarial benign), plus 2,000-example test set for Defense Success Rate
CSQA / CommonsenseQA (Talmor et al. 2019) - 1,221-example test set
DAN (Shen et al. 2024) - 1,405-example jailbreak prompt set
quantitative_results: Llama: CAST-SFT (Safe Zone) MMLU 55.73 vs Full-SFT MMLU 46.28 (+9.45 pts), Avg Safe 92.62 vs 90.61 (Table 1)
Mistral: CAST-SFT (Risky Zone) MMLU collapses to 27.78 / CSQA to 25.72 vs Safe Zone MMLU 47.62 / CSQA 56.35 (Table 1)
Predictive validity MMLU-CR: Pearson r = 0.99 (Llama), 1.00 (Qwen), 0.94 (Mistral); Spearman rho = 1.00 for all three (Figure 5)
Predictive validity UCR: Pearson r = 0.95 (Llama), 0.73 (Qwen), 0.95 (Mistral); Spearman rho = 1.00 (Llama, Mistral), 0.80 (Qwen) (Figure 6)
CAST+PCGrad (Safe Zone) synergy example: Mistral MMLU rescued from 27.78% (Risky Zone, PCGrad) to 51.43% (Risky+PCGrad) and 53.46% at Safe Zone peak (Section 5.2)
Qwen Gen. Avg gap CAST-PCG Safe Zone vs Risky Zone: +18.51 points (Section 5.2)
Ablation: unified C(h) maintains Pearson r in [0.94, 1.00] for MMLU-CR where decoupled O-only/S-only metrics fail (e.g. S: r=-0.10 on Qwen; O: r=-0.13 on Llama MMLU-CR) (Table 8 / Appendix F.1)
Computational overhead: ~1 minute per head (gradient + ablation) on 100 samples, benchmarked on NVIDIA H800 (Section 5.4)
baselines_compared: Full SFT - standard LoRA fine-tuning applied to all attention heads (global baseline)
PCGrad (Yu et al. 2020) - gradient-projection multi-task optimization baseline, applied globally and also combined with CAST head selection
Random Selection (25% of heads) - budget-matched control to isolate the effect of which heads are chosen vs how many
